transcript.text: I'm an automated medical assistant. If this is an emergency, hang up and call 911. Do you consent to continue? (yes/no)
Yes.
What is your main medical concern today?
I have lower abdominal pain.
When did your lower abdominal pain start?
4 days ago.
Can you describe the nature of the pain and if it radiates anywhere?
It's sharp and stabbing, but doesn't radiate anywhere.
Any fever, chills, or nausea since the pain started?
Yes. I have fever, chills, and nausea.
Have you noticed any changes in your bowel movements, such as diarrhea or constipation, or any blood in your stool?
No.
Have you experienced any urinary symptoms such as pain with urination, increased frequency, or blood in your urine?
No.
Any vaginal symptoms such as abnormal discharge, bleeding, or pain with intercourse?
I've been bleeding a little bit more.
Can you tell me more about the vaginal bleeding, such as how much more bleeding you have and if it is between periods or heavier than your normal period?
It's just heavier than my normal period.
Are you currently pregnant or could you be pregnant?
No.
Any pain or tenderness when you press on your lower abdomen?
Yes.
Have you had any similar episodes of lower abdominal pain with fever and increased bleeding in the past?
No.
The next few questions may feel repetitive, but they are important for medication safety and treatment decisions. How old are you? Please say your age in years.
Do you have any medication allergies? If yes, list them. If no, say no.
Penicillin.
What sex were you assigned at birth? Please say male or female.
Female.
Is pregnancy possible? (yes/no/unknown)
No.
Have you ever been told you have kidney disease or significantly reduced kidney function? (yes/no/not sure)
No.
Have you ever been told you have liver disease (such as hepatitis, cirrhosis, fatty liver, or abnormal liver tests)? (yes/no/not sure)
No.

Based on your symptoms, you most likely have an infection in your pelvic area, such as pelvic inflammatory disease. Because this is a phone visit, I can’t examine you or run tests, but medication treatment may be appropriate. A licensed physician will review this note and, if appropriate, may prescribe antibiotics to help clear the infection. In the meantime, if your pain worsens, your fever stays high or gets worse, heavy vaginal bleeding continues, or you develop new symptoms, please seek in-person care urgently. If you experience severe abdominal pain or signs of serious infection like confusion, difficulty breathing, or very high fever, go to the emergency room right away. A doctor will review your case soon and decide on the best treatment. If you need an in-person exam or testing, please visit urgent care or your primary care clinician.
